section: Pocket Card: Management Algorithm and Clinical Sidebars
text: Lipid Management for Cardiovascular • Increase physical activity (aerobic and resistance exercise) • MI or ACS Sidebar 6: For Statin Intolerance** Disease Risk Reduction that maximizes what the patient is willing and able to achieve • CABG/PCI 1. Washout period (e.g., 1 month) followed by the same or a Management Algorithm* • The stated goals of minutes per week are 150 minutes • Stable CAD different statin; continue other lipid-lowering therapy of moderate-intensity physical activity OR 75 minutes • CVA/TIA due to atherosclerosis 2. Lower dose or nondaily dosing (e.g., every other day or 2-3 days of vigorous-intensity physical activity OR an • PAD per week) of statin (see Recommendation 18) equivalent combination. • Does not include asymptomatic atherosclerosis on imaging 3. Consider initiating bempedoic acid, ezetimibe, fibrates, or PCSK9 • Choose a healthy dietary pattern (e.g., Mediterranean diet) (e.g., CCTA, CAC, catheterization) mAb inhibitors in patients unable to take a statin (see • Sleep 7-8 hours/night Recommendation 19) • Socialize: forge and embrace social connections • Quit using tobacco and nicotine Sidebar 4: Very High-Risk CVD Patients Sidebar 7: Novel Risk Markers • Minimize alcohol consumption • Manage stress • MI or ACS in past 12 months on lipid-lowering therapy; or • Suggest checking Lp(a) to identify intrinsic enhanced risk (see • Address overweight and obesity • Recurrent ACS, MI, or atherosclerotic CVA on lipid-lowering Recommendation 5) therapy; or • Not recommended to routinely measure CAC in patients with low • ASCVD and LDL-C ≥70 mg/dL on lipid-lowering therapy risk (see Recommendation 4) Sidebar 2: Mediterranean and Other Cardioprotective • Suggest CAC measurement in patients with intermediate to high Diets risk who question the need for therapy (see Recommendation 3) Sidebar 5: Statin Intensity Emphasize Limit • The routine measurement of hs-CRP, ApoB, PRS, TPA, or ABI is not useful to refine risk (see Recommendation 6) Generic Name Moderate Intensity High Intensity Fruits and vegetables Added sugar Sidebar 8: Elevated Triglycerides for Secondary CVD Rosuvastatin 5 – 10 mg 20 – 40 mg Prevention Whole grains Sugar-sweetened beverages • Consider secondary causes of elevated triglycerides† Atorvastatin 10 – 20 mg 40 – 80 mg • If triglycerides are persistently elevated (≥150 mg/dL) despite Seafood (primarily fatty fish) Sodium maximally tolerated statin, then consider icosapent ethyl 2 g BID (see Recommendation 16) Skinless poultry Highly processed foods 80 mg (XL) or • Modify diet Fluvastatin N/A 40 mg BID Tree nuts, seeds, peanuts, nut Refined carbohydrates Abbreviations: ABI: ankle brachial index; ApoB: apolipoprotein B; ACS: acute butters Lovastatin 40 – 80 mg N/A coronary syndrome; ASCVD: atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease; BID: twice per day; CABG: coronary artery bypass grafting; CAC: coronary artery Beans and legumes Saturated fats calcium; CAD: coronary artery disease; CCTA: coronary computed Pitavastatin 1 – 4 mg N/A tomography angiography; CVA: cerebrovascular accident; CVD: Non-tropical vegetable oils Tropical vegetable oils cardiovascular disease; dL: deciliter; DM: diabetes mellitus; g: gram; hs-CRP: (olive, canola, avocado, etc.) (coconut, palm, etc.) Pravastatin 40 – 80 mg N/A high-sensitivity C-reactive protein; LDL-C: low-density lipoprotein cholesterol; Lp(a): lipoprotein(a); mAb: monoclonal antibody; mg: milligram; MI: myocardial Low-fat diary products (milk, infarction; PAD: peripheral arterial disease; PCI: percutaneous coronary High-fat and processed meats Simvastatin 20 – 40 mg N/A cheese) intervention; PCSK9: proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9; PREVENT: Predicting Risk of Cardiovascular Disease Events; PRS: polygenic risk scores; *Values for estimated risk are based on the PREVENT risk assessment Intensified patient care (e.g., phone calls, emails, patient TIA: transient ischemic attack; TPA: total carotid plaque area; XL: sustained tool. Alcoholic beverages education, drug regimen simplification) may improve adherence to release lipid-lowering medications. Recommendations can be accessed in the full guideline: ** Other groups have described in more detail additional management strategies for statin intolerance; for example: the VA’s Management of Statin https://www.healthquality.va.gov/. Intolerance Clinician Fact Sheet IB 10-1695; † Secondary causes defined as co-occurring conditions, alcoholic intake, and medications that can contribute to elevated triglycerides (e.g., hormones, immune-related, beta blockers, thiazide/loop diuretics, bile acid sequestrants, atypical antipsychotics, isotretinoin).
